the fractional part of $ x $, denoted by $ \{x\} $, is the part of $ x $ after the decimal point. for example, $ \{2.5\} = 0.5 $, and $ \{ -1.2\} = 0.8 $ because the fractional part is always non-negative. the inequality given is:

$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$

this inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, and so on, up to $ x^{99} $. the goal is to determine whether there exists a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer and satisfies this inequality.

the fractional part of any real number is always between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). for $ x > 1 $, the fractional parts of $ x^k $ will also be between 0 and 1, and their sum will be less than $ k $, where $ k $ is the highest power of $ x $ in the sum.

since the inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, and so on, up to $ x^{99} $, we can analyze the behavior of the fractional parts of these powers of $ x $.

for $ x > 1 $, the fractional parts of $ x^k $ will be affected by the fractional part of $ x $ itself. if $ \{x\} = f $, then $ \{x^k\} $ will be related to $ f $ through the properties of fractional parts. for example, $ \{x^2\} = \{x \cdot x\} $, and this can be expressed in terms of $ f $ and the integer part of $ x $.

since the sum of the fractional parts of $ x^k $ is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, this implies that the sum of the fractional parts of $ x^k $ must be very small. this is only possible if the fractional parts of $ x^k $ are all very close to 0.
